question.text: Which of the following makes an issuer INELIGIBLE for an IPO under Regulation 5 of SEBI ICDR?

answer.text: The issuer has outstanding ESOPs that are fully paid up
The issuer has net tangible assets below Rs. 3 crore
A promoter of the issuer is a wilful defaulter
The issuer has changed its name in the preceding year

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Regulation 5 explicitly bars an IPO if the promoter or director of the issuer is a wilful defaulter or a fraudulent borrower. Other ineligibility grounds include: issuer/promoters/directors debarred by SEBI, being a promoter/director of a debarred company, or being a fugitive economic offender. **The Trap:** Net tangible assets below Rs. 3 crore is an eligibility condition under Regulation 6(1), not an ineligibility condition under Regulation 5. The issuer may still use the alternative QIB route. Outstanding ESOPs are specifically exempted from the convertible securities bar. **Cross-Exam Context:** Wilful defaulter provisions are tested across NISM Series IX and also appear in banking-related NISM certifications, reflecting RBI and SEBI coordination on credit discipline. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** During the initial due diligence phase, always check the RBI wilful defaulter list and SEBI debarment orders for all promoters and directors — discovering these issues late can derail an entire mandate.
